source_paper_title: The Competition Complexity of Dynamic Pricing
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Brustle_2024, title={The Competition Complexity of Dynamic Pricing}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0230}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0230}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Brustle, Johannes and Correa, José and Duetting, Paul and Verdugo, Victor}, year={2024}, month=aug, pages={1986–2008} }
context:
Page 9, Section 2.5 (end of Theorem 4 discussion)

Our lower bound for the Competition Complexity in Theorem 4 uses \(m − 1\) MHR distributions and one regular distribution. It remains open whether the Competition Complexity is sublinear for the case of \(m\) independent and MHR stages.
title: Determine sublinear competition complexity for independent monotone hazard rate stages
background_latex:
Consider a dynamic auction with 
1) a single seller, 
2) a fixed set of buyers present in every stage, and 
3) one item sold in each of \(m\) consecutive stages.

In each stage \(k\in\{1,\dots,m\}\), each buyer \(i\in\{1,\dots,n\}\) draws a private value \(v_i^k\) for that stage's item. Values are independent across buyers. Stages are independent in the sense that, for each buyer, the random variables \(v_i^1,\dots,v_i^m\) are independent across \(k\). The (common, i.i.d. across buyers) marginal distribution at stage \(k\) is a continuous distribution \(D_k\) on \(\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\) with cumulative distribution function \(F_k\) and density \(f_k\).

A continuous distribution \(D\) with cdf \(F\) and density \(f\) is monotone hazard rate (MHR) if its hazard rate
\[
 h(x)=\frac{f(x)}{1-F(x)}
\]
is nondecreasing in \(x\) on its support.

Let \(\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m]\) denote the optimal expected revenue achievable by a revenue-maximizing incentive-compatible dynamic mechanism with \(n\) buyers over \(m\) stages when the full stochastic environment is \(X\) (here, independent stages with marginals \(D_1,\dots,D_m\)). Let \(\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c,m]\) denote the expected revenue of running a second-price auction (VCG for a single item) separately in each stage with \(n+c\) buyers, where the extra \(c\) buyers are also i.i.d. from the same stage distributions and participate in every stage.

The competition complexity (with respect to stagewise VCG) is the minimum \(c\ge 0\) such that
\[
\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c,m]\;\ge\;\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m].
\]
(Equivalently, the smallest number of additional buyers needed so that simple stagewise second-price auctions match the optimal dynamic auction's expected revenue.)
problem_definition_latex:
In the setting above with independent stages and continuous MHR marginals \(D_1,\dots,D_m\), determine the asymptotic growth (in \(n\)) of the competition complexity \(c\) needed for stagewise VCG to achieve
\[
\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c,m]\;\ge\;\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m].
\]
In particular, decide whether there exists a function \(c(n)=o(n)\) (possibly depending on \(m\) but not on the specific MHR distributions) such that for every choice of continuous MHR stage distributions \(D_1,\dots,D_m\) with independent stages,
\[
\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c(n),m]\;\ge\;\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m].
\]
latex:
Consider a dynamic auction with 
1) a single seller, 
2) a fixed set of buyers present in every stage, and 
3) one item sold in each of \(m\) consecutive stages.

In each stage \(k\in\{1,\dots,m\}\), each buyer \(i\in\{1,\dots,n\}\) draws a private value \(v_i^k\) for that stage's item. Values are independent across buyers. Stages are independent in the sense that, for each buyer, the random variables \(v_i^1,\dots,v_i^m\) are independent across \(k\). The (common, i.i.d. across buyers) marginal distribution at stage \(k\) is a continuous distribution \(D_k\) on \(\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\) with cumulative distribution function \(F_k\) and density \(f_k\).

A continuous distribution \(D\) with cdf \(F\) and density \(f\) is monotone hazard rate (MHR) if its hazard rate
\[
 h(x)=\frac{f(x)}{1-F(x)}
\]
is nondecreasing in \(x\) on its support.

Let \(\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m]\) denote the optimal expected revenue achievable by a revenue-maximizing incentive-compatible dynamic mechanism with \(n\) buyers over \(m\) stages when the full stochastic environment is \(X\) (here, independent stages with marginals \(D_1,\dots,D_m\)). Let \(\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c,m]\) denote the expected revenue of running a second-price auction (VCG for a single item) separately in each stage with \(n+c\) buyers, where the extra \(c\) buyers are also i.i.d. from the same stage distributions and participate in every stage.

The competition complexity (with respect to stagewise VCG) is the minimum \(c\ge 0\) such that
\[
\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c,m]\;\ge\;\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m].
\]
(Equivalently, the smallest number of additional buyers needed so that simple stagewise second-price auctions match the optimal dynamic auction's expected revenue.)

In the setting above with independent stages and continuous MHR marginals \(D_1,\dots,D_m\), determine the asymptotic growth (in \(n\)) of the competition complexity \(c\) needed for stagewise VCG to achieve
\[
\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c,m]\;\ge\;\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m].
\]
In particular, decide whether there exists a function \(c(n)=o(n)\) (possibly depending on \(m\) but not on the specific MHR distributions) such that for every choice of continuous MHR stage distributions \(D_1,\dots,D_m\) with independent stages,
\[
\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c(n),m]\;\ge\;\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m].
\]
open_problem_quote: Our lower bound for the Competition Complexity in Theorem 4 uses \(m − 1\) MHR distributions and one regular distribution. It remains open whether the Competition Complexity is sublinear for the case of \(m\) independent and MHR stages.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can computationally search over parametric families of continuous MHR distributions (e.g., piecewise-exponential hazards) and simulate VCG and candidate upper bounds on \(\mathrm{OPT}\) to look for instances that force \(c\) to be linear. However, exactly computing \(\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m]\) for dynamic mechanisms is generally hard, so the search would rely on relaxations/upper bounds, limiting decisiveness.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary loop could represent stage distributions via hazard-rate parametrizations and attempt to maximize an estimated gap between \(\mathrm{OPT}\) (approximated via LP discretizations or known upper bounds) and stagewise VCG revenue with \(n+c\) buyers, providing a computable fitness signal. The main limitation is that faithfully evaluating \(\mathrm{OPT}\) is computationally expensive and approximation-dependent, so the signal may be noisy or only indicative.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem from Liu–Psomas asks whether, for independent stages with continuous MHR marginals \(D_1,\dots,D_m\), the competition complexity of running stagewise VCG to match the optimal dynamic mechanism can be made sublinear in \(n\) uniformly over all such marginals. The source paper establishes linear upper and lower bounds in more general settings: for (possibly correlated) MHR marginals it proves competition complexity between \((e-1)n\) and \(3n\) using the social-welfare upper bound and new inequalities comparing order statistics of MHR distributions (notably \(\mathbb E[X_{2:4n}]\ge \mathbb E[X_{1:n}]\)). For independent stages with \(m-1\) MHR and one merely regular stage, it still obtains the same linear bounds via a dynamic extension of the Cai–Devanur–Weinberg duality framework (Lemma 1) plus Bulow–Klemperer for the regular stage. The remaining gap is precisely the all-stages-independent-and-MHR case: the paper’s lower bound construction for exact optimality uses one regular (non-MHR) stage, leaving open whether MHR everywhere might permit \(o(n)\) extra buyers.

Subsequent forward citations do not settle this question. The closest negative evidence comes indirectly from static settings: “Strengthening Bulow-Klemperer-Style Results for Multi-Unit Auctions” shows that even with i.i.d. MHR values, exact competition complexity for VCG in multi-unit auctions can be linear in \(n\) (e.g. \(\approx (e^{1/e}-1)n\) in balanced markets), suggesting that MHR alone does not preclude linear worst-case competition requirements. Meanwhile, “Settling the competition complexity of additive buyers over independent items” pins down \(\Theta(\sqrt{nm})\) competition complexity in a static multi-item additive model under regularity, demonstrating that independence across components does not automatically yield small competition complexity; however its hard instances are truncated equal-revenue and thus not MHR.

Other citing works develop tools around resource augmentation in prophet inequalities (LP duality, extreme-value asymptotics, and sharp \((1-\varepsilon)\)-competition complexity for threshold policies). These are methodologically adjacent—sharing order-statistic and large-market analyses—but they address welfare/prophet benchmarks rather than revenue and do not compare stagewise VCG revenue to optimal dynamic-auction revenue under MHR. Overall, the dynamic MHR-independent-stages exact competition complexity question appears to remain open; a promising direction is to seek either (i) a reduction from known static MHR lower bounds (e.g. multi-unit) to the dynamic setting to rule out \(o(n)\), or (ii) a refined duality-based upper bound for \(\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m]\) under independent MHR stages that can be matched by \(\mathbb E[X_{2:n+c}]\) with sublinear \(c\), potentially leveraging sharper MHR order-statistic comparisons beyond the \(4n\)-vs-\(n\) bound used in the source paper.

sources.title: Settling the competition complexity of additive buyers over independent items
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves tight competition complexity (\(\sqrt{nm}\)) for selling \(m<n\) independent items to \(n\) additive bidders: for all regular independent-item distributions, the number of extra bidders needed so that selling separately/VCG with \(n+c\) bidders beats the optimal BIC mechanism with \(n\) bidders is \(c=\Theta(\sqrt{nm})\) (big-\(n\) regime), witnessed by truncated equal-revenue distributions \(\mathrm{ER}_{\le T}\) with \(T=\Theta(\sqrt{nm})\). In particular, they construct an explicit BIC mechanism for \((\mathrm{ER}_{\le T})^{n\times m}\) whose revenue exceeds \(\mathrm{SRev}_{n+c\sqrt{nm}}(\mathrm{ER}_{\le T}^m)\), implying a lower bound \(\Omega(\sqrt{nm})\), matching the \(O(\sqrt{nm})\) upper bound of Beyhaghi–Weinberg (2019).
sources.connection: The open problem concerns dynamic auctions over \(m\) stages with independent MHR stage distributions \(D_k\), asking whether stagewise VCG with \(n+o(n)\) buyers can always match the optimal dynamic mechanism. This paper instead studies a static multi-item setting (\(m\) independent items sold once) with additive bidders and derives \(\Theta(\sqrt{nm})\) competition complexity for selling items separately. While the mathematical structure (independent components, competition complexity vs. optimal BIC) is analogous if one views stages as items, the valuation model differs: in the dynamic problem each buyer has per-stage values but no additivity across stages in utility (they only win/pay in that stage), and the benchmark is the optimal dynamic mechanism (with intertemporal incentives), not the optimal multi-item additive mechanism. Also, the papers hard instances use truncated equal-revenue distributions, which are not MHR. Therefore it does not resolve the MHR dynamic-pricing question, but it provides techniques and cautionary evidence about worst-case distributions and the size of competition needed in related independent-component settings.

sources.title: Posted Pricing and Competition in Large Markets
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper studies i.i.d. single-item posted pricing/prophet-inequality benchmarks in a large-market regime (distribution fixed, number of buyers/arrivals \,\(n\to\infty\)). It defines a large-market competition complexity for the optimal dynamic programming (prophet) policy relative to the expected maximum \(\mathbb E[M_n]\): \(C(F)=\liminf_{n\to\infty}\inf_{m\ge n}\{m/n: G_m(F)\ge \mathbb E[M_n]\}\), where \(G_{n+1}(F)=\mathbb E[\max\{X,G_n(F)\}]\). For distributions satisfying the extreme value condition with extreme value index \(\gamma\), it proves \(C(F)=(1-\gamma)(\Gamma(1-\gamma))^{1/\gamma}\); in particular \(C(F)=e^{\gamma_\star}\approx 1.781\) for the Gumbel case (\(\gamma=0\)).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for sublinear (ideally \(o(n)\)) competition complexity for *stagewise VCG* in a *multi-stage dynamic auction* with independent stages and MHR marginals \(D_1,\dots,D_m\), comparing \(\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},n+c,m]\) to the optimal dynamic mechanism revenue \(\mathrm{OPT}[n,m]\). This paper does not analyze multi-stage auctions, does not consider VCG/second-price revenue, and does not assume MHR; instead it analyzes single-stage i.i.d. posted pricing / prophet inequalities under an extreme-value-condition and derives a constant-factor *multiplicative* scaling of sample size needed for the optimal dynamic programming policy to match \(\mathbb E[M_n]\). Thus it does not resolve the stated question, but it is tangentially related via the broader theme of competition complexity in dynamic pricing and shows that worst-case unboundedness can disappear under a large-market assumption (for a different benchmark and mechanism class).

sources.title: Strengthening Bulow-Klemperer-Style Results for Multi-Unit Auctions
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves Bulow–Klemperer-style competition-complexity bounds for static multi-unit auctions with i.i.d. values from MHR (more generally, \(\lambda\)-regular) distributions. In particular, for balanced \(m=n\) markets with MHR values, it shows
\[\Big(e^{1/e}-1\Big)n \le CC(n,\mathrm{MHR}) \le \left\lceil \Big(e^{1/e}-1\Big)n + 1.05\ln n\right\rceil,\]
where \(CC(n,\mathrm{MHR})\) is the minimum number of extra i.i.d. buyers needed so that the \(n\)-unit VCG auction’s expected revenue is at least the Bayesian-optimal revenue in the original \(n\)-buyer market (and it gives analogous asymptotic formulas for general \(m\le n\) and \(\Gamma<1\)).
sources.connection:
The open problem concerns a *dynamic* auction over \(m\) independent stages with stagewise MHR marginals \(D_k\), asking whether stagewise VCG with \(n+c(n)\) buyers can match the optimal *dynamic* mechanism with only sublinear \(c(n)=o(n)\) uniformly over all MHR stage distributions.

This citing paper does not analyze dynamic mechanisms or intertemporal incentive constraints; it studies *static* multi-unit auctions (one-shot sale of \(m\) identical units) with i.i.d. values. Its results therefore do not resolve whether dynamic optimal revenue can be matched by per-stage VCG with sublinear extra buyers.

At best, it provides related tools/benchmarks: it shows that even in static settings with MHR, competition complexity for exact optimality can be linear in \(n\) (e.g., \(\approx 0.4447n\) in balanced markets). This suggests that expecting \(o(n)\) extra buyers in the dynamic setting may be impossible in worst case, but no formal reduction from the dynamic problem is given here.

sources.title: Tightness without counterexamples: A new approach and new results for prophet inequalities
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops an LP-duality framework (“Type Coverage” dual) that characterizes tight prophet-inequality ratios for k-unit selection, including tight bounds for static threshold policies and numerical characterization of the tight DP/prophet ratio in the IID case. In particular, for IID k-unit selection it shows \(\mathrm{DP}/\mathrm{ExAnte}=\sup_{\tau}\mathrm{OST}(\tau)/\mathrm{Proph}=\mathbb{E}[\min\{\mathrm{Bin}(n,k/n),k\}]/k\) (Theorem 8), and for non-IID static thresholds the tight guarantee is \(1-\Theta(\sqrt{\log k/k})\) (Proposition 3).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for sublinear competition complexity for stagewise VCG to match the optimal dynamic mechanism in a multi-stage auction with independent MHR marginals. This paper is about prophet inequalities (online selection vs prophet/ex-ante benchmarks) and does not analyze dynamic auctions, VCG revenue, or competition complexity. However, prophet-inequality techniques and ex-ante/LP duality sometimes underlie revenue-approximation and competition-complexity arguments (e.g., via posted-price/threshold policies). Thus the paper may provide methodological tools (LP duality, type-coverage constraints) that could be adapted in future work, but it does not address the stated competition-complexity question or MHR-specific guarantees.

sources.title: Splitting guarantees for prophet inequalities via nonlinear systems
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper studies the i.i.d. kk-selection prophet inequality (select up to kk items from nn i.i.d. draws) and gives an exact infinite-dimensional LP characterization of the optimal worst-case approximation ratio \(\gamma_{n,k}\), plus an asymptotic lower bound via a nonlinear ODE system. Concretely, for each fixed k\ge 1\) there exist constants \(\theta_1^\star,\dots,\theta_k^\star\) (defined by solvability of a coupled differential system (1)–(3)) and \(n_0(k)\) such that for all \(n\ge n_0(k)\),
\[\gamma_{n,k}\ge \Bigl(1-\tfrac{24k\ln(n)^2}{n}\Bigr)\sum_{j=1}^k \theta_j^\star.\]
Equivalently, it constructs a feasible solution to a weak dual LP yielding the above guarantee (Theorem 2).
sources.connection: The open problem concerns competition complexity of stagewise VCG versus the optimal dynamic mechanism in a multi-stage auction with independent stages and MHR marginals, asking whether o(\(n\)) extra bidders suffice uniformly over MHR distributions. This paper does not analyze dynamic auctions, VCG revenue, MHR distributions, or competition complexity. It instead develops LP/ODE techniques for worst-case prophet-inequality approximation ratios in i.i.d. selection problems. While prophet-inequality methods sometimes relate to posted pricing and competition complexity, no theorem here implies sublinear competition complexity for the stated dynamic pricing/auction setting, nor does it address MHR stage distributions or the revenue comparison \(\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},\cdot]\ge \mathrm{OPT}[\cdot]\). At best, it provides potentially relevant analytical tools (infinite-dimensional LP dual fitting; nonlinear ODE systems) that might inspire future bounds in sequential mechanism settings.

sources.title: The competition complexity of prophet inequalities
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper defines and tightly characterizes the \((1-\varepsilon)\)-competition complexity for the classic single-choice prophet inequality under resource augmentation (\(k\) i.i.d. copies/blocks). Main theorems: (i) For block-threshold algorithms, \((1-\varepsilon)\)-competition complexity is \(\Theta(\log\log(1/\varepsilon))\) (Theorem 1). (ii) For single-threshold algorithms it is \(\Theta(\log(1/\varepsilon))\) (Theorem 2). The analysis is via an approximate stochastic dominance lemma for block thresholds (Lemma 1) and an explicit quantile/threshold construction giving factor \(1-(5/4)^{-(5/4)^k}\) (Lemma 2).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for sublinear (in \(n\)) competition complexity \(c(n)=o(n)\) for stagewise VCG to match the optimal dynamic mechanism in a multi-stage auction with independent MHR stage distributions. This paper does not study dynamic auctions, VCG revenue, or MHR assumptions; it studies prophet inequalities (single-choice stopping) and welfare-style objectives under sample augmentation. However, it develops general techniques—resource-augmentation/competition-complexity viewpoint, block-threshold optimality, and approximate stochastic dominance bounds—that are conceptually related to Bulow–Klemperer-type questions and to dynamic pricing interpretations. These tools might inspire analogous analyses for dynamic auction revenue benchmarks, but no direct bound on \(c(n)\) for MHR stages is provided.

sources.title: Multiunit IID Prophet Inequalities via Extreme Value Asymptotics
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper studies the i.i.d. k-selection prophet inequality (online selection of up to k rewards from n i.i.d. draws) under an extreme-value condition. It characterizes the asymptotic competitive ratio of the optimal online dynamic program versus the prophet as n→∞: if the reward distribution has extreme value index γ, then
- for γ∈(0,1): \(\mathrm{ACR}_k(F)= (1-\gamma)^{1-\gamma} v_k\,\Gamma(k)/\Gamma(k+1-\gamma)\), where \(v_1=1\) and \(v_k-v_{k-1}\) solves \(x^{1/\gamma}+v_{k-1}x^{1/\gamma-1}-1=0\);
- otherwise \(\mathrm{ACR}_k(F)=1\).
It further gives a large-k expansion (γ∈(0,1)): \(\mathrm{ACR}_k(F)=1-\frac{\gamma(1-\gamma)}{2}\frac{\log k}{k}+O(1/k)\), implying a worst-case lower bound \(\mathrm{ACR}_k(F)\ge 1-\frac{\log k}{8k}(1+\varepsilon)\) for large k.
sources.connection: The open problem concerns competition complexity in a multi-stage dynamic auction with n buyers per stage and MHR stage distributions, asking whether adding o(n) extra buyers makes stagewise VCG revenue match the optimal dynamic mechanism’s revenue uniformly over MHR marginals. This paper does not analyze dynamic auctions, VCG revenue, or competition complexity directly. It instead analyzes a different but related online-selection/profit-inequality model (k-unit selection from n i.i.d. draws) using extreme value asymptotics, and briefly notes implications for “competition complexity” in a concluding discussion (not provided here). The technical results could be conceptually relevant as tools for large-market asymptotics and order-statistic behavior, but they do not establish sublinear competition complexity for independent MHR stages, nor do they compare stagewise VCG with optimal dynamic auction revenue.

sources.title: Competition Versus Complexity in Multiple-Selection Prophet Inequalities
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper fully characterizes the approximate competition complexity for i.i.d. multiple-selection prophet inequalities within the class of single-threshold algorithms. In particular, it gives an exact formula for the optimal (m,n,k)-competitive ratio 
\[\gamma_{m,n,k}=\frac{Q_{m,k}(k/n)}{k}=\frac{1}{k}\Bigl(k-\sum_{\ell=0}^{k-1}(k-\ell)\binom{m}{\ell}(k/n)^\ell(1-k/n)^{m-\ell}\Bigr),\]
where \(Q_{m,k}(q)=\mathbb{E}[\min\{k,Y\}]\) for \(Y\sim\mathrm{Binomial}(m,q)\). It also derives bounds on the (1−\(\varepsilon\),k)-competition complexity \(\beta_k(\varepsilon)\): for \(k=1\), \(\beta_1(\varepsilon)=\ln(1/\varepsilon)\); for \(k>1\), \(\ln(1/\varepsilon)/k\le \beta_k(\varepsilon)\le 1+\frac{2\ln(1/\varepsilon)}{k-1}+\sqrt{\frac{2\ln(1/\varepsilon)}{k-1}}\).
sources.connection: The open problem concerns revenue competition complexity in a dynamic auction with independent stages and MHR marginals, asking whether stagewise VCG with \(n+c(n)\) buyers can match the optimal dynamic mechanism with \(n\) buyers using sublinear \(c(n)=o(n)\), uniformly over MHR stage distributions. This citing paper does not study dynamic auctions, MHR assumptions, or VCG revenue; it studies welfare maximization in i.i.d. prophet inequalities and competition complexity for single-threshold (posted-price) algorithms versus a prophet benchmark. The connection is indirect: prophet-inequality/posted-price techniques are often used as tools in mechanism design and dynamic pricing, and the paper develops an infinite-dimensional LP/duality framework to exactly compute competition complexity for a simple policy class. However, its results do not imply sublinear buyer-addition guarantees for stagewise VCG revenue in the multi-stage MHR auction model.
